ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific term for stewed pear soup. The pear commodity code A00LC is used as the closest parent. The prepared-dish level is not represented in FoodEx2 for this item.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for stewed pear soup as a prepared food item.

main_ingredients: snow pear (Pyrus nivalis or Nashi pear)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g (one bowl), which is typical for a Chinese sweet soup served as a dessert or therapeutic beverage in Singapore hawker and restaurant settings.

nutrient_source.title: Pears, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169118); adapted for stewed preparation with rock sugar addition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for stewed pear soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw pear data (FDC ID 169118), adjusted for stewing dilution (approximately 60% pear solids by weight in finished soup) and rock sugar addition (approximately 8 g sugar per 100 g soup). Water content is increased to reflect the aqueous soup medium. Energy and macros are recalculated accordingly. This is an estimated composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; pear (fresh) GI = 38
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stewed pear soup as a prepared dish. The GI of fresh pear (GI = 38, low) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) is used as the closest analogous estimate. Stewing softens the pear and may marginally raise the GI; however, the high water content and dilution of sugars in the soup medium are expected to keep the GI in the low range. GL per serving calculated as: (38 × 10.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 = 9.975, rounded to 9.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (approximately 3 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Stewed pear soup is very low in sodium (approximately 7.5 mg per 250 g serving) and poses minimal concern for individuals managing hypertension; it can be consumed freely as part of a low-sodium dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content from rock sugar addition and natural pear sugars; low GI (estimated 38)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI is low (estimated 38), the added rock sugar contributes approximately 9.8 g of sugar per 100 g; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume stewed pear soup in moderate portions and consider requesting reduced or no added rock sugar to limit glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; low sodium; contains dietary fibre and antioxidants from pear
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stewed pear soup is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol and provides modest dietary fibre and antioxidant compounds from pear, making it a heart-friendly beverage choice when prepared without excessive added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stewed Pear Soup (炖梨汤 / 燉梨湯; Dùn Lí Tāng) is a traditional Chinese sweet soup and therapeutic beverage widely consumed in China, Singapore, and across the Chinese diaspora. It is prepared by slow-simmering or double-boiling whole or halved snow pears (雪梨, xuě lí) — typically the Nashi or Chinese white pear variety — in water with rock sugar (冰糖) until the pear becomes tender and the broth is lightly sweetened and fragrant. Optional additions include snow fungus (银耳, yín ěr), red dates (红枣, hóng zǎo), wolfberries / goji berries (枸杞, gǒu qǐ), and dried longan (龙眼, lóng yǎn), each contributing additional sweetness, texture, and traditional medicinal properties. The resulting soup is clear to pale golden in colour, mildly sweet, and delicately flavoured with the natural floral notes of pear. In Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), stewed pear soup is prized for its purported lung-moistening and throat-soothing properties and is commonly consumed during dry weather, respiratory illness, or as a post-meal dessert. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-fat beverage with moderate natural sugar content, modest dietary fibre, and a low glycaemic index (estimated GI 38 based on fresh pear analogue). It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. In Singapore, it is served in Chinese restaurants, hawker centres, and dessert stalls, and is available on food delivery platforms. Clinically, it is low in sodium and saturated fat, making it broadly suitable for individuals managing hypertension and cardiovascular disease; those with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size and request reduced added sugar.
common_user_queries: Is stewed pear soup good for diabetics?
Pear soup for cough and sore throat

confidence_reason: No direct entry for stewed pear soup as a prepared dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw pear data with adjustments for stewing dilution and rock sugar addition. GI is estimated from the fresh pear analogue (University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated by adapting USDA SR Legacy raw pear data (FDC ID 169118) for a stewed soup preparation: approximately 60% pear solids by weight, with rock sugar contributing approximately 8 g sugar per 100 g of finished soup, and water content raised to approximately 88.5 g per 100 g. These are modelled estimates and should be replaced with direct laboratory analysis if available. 2. saturated_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; pears contain negligible saturated fat. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as pears and the other plant ingredients in this soup contain no vitamin D. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.02) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.03) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pear fatty acid profile, scaled for dilution. 4. GI is estimated at 38 based on fresh pear analogue from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Stewing may marginally raise GI but the high water dilution is expected to maintain a low GI category. GL per 250 g serving = (38 × 10.5 × 250) / 10000 = 9.975, rounded to 9.9. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches only; no exact prepared-dish ontology term exists for stewed pear soup. 6. Dietary flags: is_kosher is set to false as kosher certification status is unknown and not verifiable from available sources. 7. The field dataed in the source data (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles 兰亭序 - Lazada One) serves this as a side beverage item; the cuisine_tags 'Noodles' in the input reflect the restaurant's primary cuisine rather than this dish's classification.
